title: Volt to Electron Volt Calculator
tagline: Convert voltage to electron volts (eV) and joules instantly for physics, electronics, and quantum mechanics.
intro: Enter a voltage in volts to see the equivalent energy in electron volts and joules. The numeric value of eV equals the voltage in V.

error.invalid: Please enter a valid positive voltage.

about.heading: About the Volt to Electron Volt Calculator
about.body:
The electron volt (eV) is the standard unit of energy used in particle physics, atomic physics, and semiconductor engineering. It is defined as the amount of kinetic energy gained by a single electron when it is accelerated through an electric potential difference of exactly one volt in a vacuum. Because the elementary charge of an electron is 1.602176634×10⁻¹⁹ coulombs, one electron volt equals 1.602176634×10⁻¹⁹ joules.

The defining equation is E = qV, where E is energy in joules, q is the charge of the particle in coulombs, and V is the voltage in volts. For a single electron, q = e (the elementary charge), so the energy gained in joules is always the voltage multiplied by 1.602176634×10⁻¹⁹. Crucially, the numeric value of the energy in electron volts is identical to the voltage in volts — 12 volts accelerates an electron to 12 eV, and 1 000 volts gives it 1 000 eV (1 keV). This elegant one-to-one mapping is what makes the electron volt such a practical unit.

Electron volts are preferred in atomic and nuclear sciences because joules are an inconveniently large unit at subatomic scales. A single photon of visible light carries only about 2–3 eV, the ionisation energy of hydrogen is 13.6 eV, a typical X-ray photon carries 100 to 100 000 eV, and gamma-ray photons exceed 100 keV. At the other end of the scale, modern particle accelerators push protons to billions of electron volts (GeV) and even trillions (TeV). All of these numbers would be unwieldy as multiples of 10⁻¹⁹ joules.

In semiconductor engineering, eV describes band gaps — the energy difference between the valence band and the conduction band of a material. Silicon has a band gap of 1.12 eV, gallium arsenide about 1.42 eV, and gallium nitride roughly 3.4 eV. These values determine which wavelengths of light a material can absorb or emit, making them essential for designing solar cells, LEDs, and laser diodes. The voltage at which a forward-biased LED turns on is approximately equal to its band gap in eV.

In electron microscopes, the accelerating voltage determines beam resolution. A 100 kV electron has a de Broglie wavelength orders of magnitude smaller than visible light, enabling sub-nanometer imaging. In mass spectrometers, the ratio of an ion's mass to its charge (m/z) is calibrated against the accelerating voltage in electron volts. Understanding the volt-to-eV relationship is therefore not merely a unit-conversion exercise — it links macroscopic electrical engineering quantities to the quantum mechanical behaviour of individual particles.

examples.heading: Volt to electron volt conversion examples
examples.intro: Common voltages and their equivalent energies in electron volts and joules.

items.note: 2.40×10⁻¹⁹ J — energy of a single electron accelerated by a fresh AA cell.
1.92×10⁻¹⁸ J — typical automotive lead-acid battery potential.
1.60×10⁻¹⁶ J — used in X-ray tubes and high-voltage electronics.

howto.heading: How to use the volt to electron volt calculator
steps: Enter the voltage value in the Voltage (V) field. Decimal and scientific-notation values are both accepted.
Click Calculate to see the equivalent energy in electron volts (eV) and in joules (J).
Note that the numeric value in eV is identical to the voltage — 5 V always equals 5 eV for a singly charged particle.
Use the example buttons below the calculator to load common voltages such as a AA battery (1.5 V) or a car battery (12 V).
Click Reset to clear the field and start a new calculation.

faq.heading: Volt to electron volt calculator FAQ

items.q: What is one electron volt equal to in joules?
items.a: One electron volt equals exactly 1.602176634×10⁻¹⁹ joules, which is the amount of energy a single electron gains when accelerated through a potential difference of one volt. This constant is fixed by the 2019 SI redefinition of the ampere and is now an exact value.

items.q: Why is the energy in eV numerically equal to the voltage?
items.a: By definition, 1 eV is the energy acquired by one elementary charge (e) through 1 volt. The formula E = qV means that for q = 1e, the energy in joules is V × e. Dividing by e to convert to eV gives simply V. So 50 volts always produces 50 eV for a singly charged particle, regardless of particle mass.

items.q: Does the formula change for particles with a charge other than one?
items.a: Yes. For a particle with charge number z (e.g., z = 2 for an alpha particle), the energy gained is z × V electron volts. A doubly ionised helium atom accelerated through 100 V gains 200 eV. The calculator assumes singly charged particles (z = 1); multiply the result by z for other charge states.

items.q: What is the difference between electron volts and volts?
items.a: Volts measure electrical potential difference (a property of the field), while electron volts measure energy (a property of the particle). They are related but distinct quantities. You cannot add 5 V to 3 eV because they have different physical dimensions; you can only say that a 5 V potential gives a singly charged particle 5 eV of kinetic energy.

items.q: How are electron volts used in semiconductor band gaps?
items.a: The band gap of a semiconductor is the minimum photon energy needed to excite an electron from the valence band to the conduction band. Silicon's band gap of 1.12 eV means a photon with at least 1.12 eV is needed. This corresponds to a wavelength of about 1 100 nm (near-infrared), which is why silicon solar cells can harvest near-IR light but not wavelengths longer than that.

items.q: Can I use this calculator for protons or other ions?
items.a: Yes for singly charged ions (z = 1), the result is the same as for electrons. For multiply charged ions such as Ca²⁺ (z = 2), double the displayed eV value. The joules result from the calculator should also be multiplied by z, since E (J) = z × e × V.
